skill.name: content-strategy
skill.description: Helps teams plan content pillars, topic clusters, buyer-stage keyword ideas, and content priorities for search and shareability.
skill.summary: Plan a focused content strategy from customer research, keyword data, and competitive gaps.
skill.author: coreyhaines31

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged Markdown backticks, ordinary text, and examples as external commands, weak cryptography, and reconnaissance. Review found no executable code, prompt injection, malware intent, credential access, or exfiltration pattern. The only real concerns are low risk: optional reading of a local context file and guidance to use web search for research.

low_findings.title: Static External Command Alerts Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The flagged backticks are Markdown inline code, URL path examples, or code fences used to describe content structures and search queries. No shell, Ruby, or command execution semantics are present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines contain Markdown formatting and plain examples, not executable Ruby or shell syntax. There is no script file or runtime path that would execute these strings.

low_findings.title: Static Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Text Matches
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The flagged lines are the skill description, research questions, keyword examples, URL path guidance, and data-source instructions. No hashing, encryption, crypto library, or weak algorithm use appears in context.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding context is content planning guidance and contains no cryptographic operation. The matches appear to come from ordinary prose or Markdown file references.

low_findings.title: Static Reconnaissance Alert Is a Customer Research Question
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The flagged line asks how often a topic appears in customer research. It does not request host details, network enumeration, operating system data, or other system reconnaissance.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is part of a content prioritization scoring rubric. It has no connection to local system or network discovery.

low_findings.title: Optional Local Context File Read
low_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill asks the assistant to read .claude/product-marketing-context.md if it exists. This is a limited filesystem access pattern, but it is relevant to the skill purpose and does not instruct exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The instruction clearly requests reading a local project file. Risk is low because the file path is specific and no network transmission or secret harvesting is requested.

low_findings.title: Web Search Guidance Requires Network Access
low_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill instructs use of web search for forum and competitor research. This is normal for content strategy, but users should expect external lookups.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The text explicitly says to use web search and provides search query examples. This confirms network use, but not malicious network behavior.

content.user_title: Plan Content Strategy That Drives Demand
content.value_statement: Many teams publish content without a clear link to customer needs or business goals. This skill turns research into pillars, topics, priorities, and formats.
actual_capabilities: Defines content pillars and topic clusters based on product, audience, search, and competitors.
Classifies content ideas as searchable, shareable, or both.
Maps keyword ideas to awareness, consideration, decision, and implementation stages.
Prioritizes topics with customer impact, product fit, search potential, and resource needs.
Extracts content ideas from keyword exports, call transcripts, surveys, forums, and competitor content.
Produces a structured strategy with pillars, priority topics, and an internal linking map.
limitations: It does not write full articles or landing pages.
It depends on the quality of customer research, keyword data, and competitor examples provided.
It does not perform technical SEO audits or site crawling.
It may need web search access for forum and competitor research.

use_cases.title: Build a blog strategy for a SaaS launch
use_cases.description: Create pillars, launch topics, and buyer-stage content ideas that support product education and lead generation.

use_cases.title: Turn customer calls into content priorities
use_cases.description: Extract questions, objections, pain points, and language patterns from transcripts to guide the editorial plan.

use_cases.title: Find gaps in competitor content
use_cases.description: Compare competitor topics, formats, and coverage depth to identify useful angles your team can own.

prompt_templates.title: Start a content strategy
prompt_templates.scenario: Begin with basic business and audience context.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me plan a content strategy for [company]. We sell [product] to [audience]. Our goal is [traffic, leads, authority, or awareness].

prompt_templates.title: Create topic clusters
prompt_templates.scenario: Organize broad content themes into pillar and spoke structures.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create 3 to 5 content pillars for [business]. For each pillar, suggest subtopics, content types, and how it connects to our product.

prompt_templates.title: Prioritize content ideas
prompt_templates.scenario: Score a backlog by impact, product fit, search potential, and resources.
prompt_templates.prompt: Prioritize these content ideas: [list]. Score each by customer impact, content-market fit, search potential, and resource requirements.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze research inputs
prompt_templates.scenario: Use keyword exports, transcripts, surveys, or competitor notes.
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this research for content strategy: [paste research]. Extract themes, buyer-stage topics, searchable ideas, shareable ideas, and priority recommendations.

output_examples.input: We sell project management software to remote product teams.
output_examples.output: A strategy with pillars for remote planning, async collaboration, sprint execution, and stakeholder reporting, plus buyer-stage topics for each pillar.

output_examples.input: Here are five sales call transcripts with repeated objections.
output_examples.output: A prioritized list of content ideas that address pricing concerns, migration risk, adoption questions, and comparison searches.

output_examples.input: Our competitors cover beginner guides, but not implementation templates.
output_examples.output: A gap-focused plan for templates, setup tutorials, use-case articles, and internal links that support product adoption.

best_practices: Provide real customer language, sales objections, and support questions before asking for topic ideas.
Separate searchable content from shareable content so each piece has a clear distribution path.
Review the final plan against team capacity before committing to a publishing schedule.
anti_patterns: Do not ask for a generic content calendar without explaining the product, audience, and goals.
Do not treat search volume as the only priority signal.
Do not use the skill as a replacement for technical SEO audits or full article writing.

faq.question: Can this skill create a full content calendar?
faq.answer: It can create the strategic inputs for a calendar, including pillars, topics, priorities, and suggested formats.

faq.question: Does it need keyword data?
faq.answer: Keyword data helps, but the skill can also use customer calls, surveys, support tickets, and competitor research.

faq.question: Can it analyze call transcripts?
faq.answer: Yes. It can extract questions, objections, pain points, competitor mentions, and useful customer language.

faq.question: Does it perform SEO audits?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on content planning. Use a dedicated SEO audit skill for technical and on-page SEO checks.

faq.question: Can it help with thought leadership?
faq.answer: Yes. It can identify shareable angles, original insights, research ideas, and experience-based topics.

faq.question: What inputs produce the best results?
faq.answer: The best inputs include product positioning, ideal customer profile, customer questions, existing content, competitors, and available resources.
